serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant or home serving of Saag Mutton is approximately 250 g, comprising a generous portion of slow-cooked mutton pieces in a thick spinach-based gravy, typically served alongside naan, paratha, or makki di roti.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Saag Mutton is a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' using component-level data for cooked mutton and spinach-based preparations. Values for the composite dish are consistent with ICMR-NIN reported ranges for similar Indian meat-and-leafy-green curries. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (mutton, cooked) as ICMR-NIN does not separately report saturated fat for this dish type.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for high-protein, high-fat, high-fibre meat-and-spinach curry
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Saag Mutton in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate based on: (1) high protein content (12.5 g/100g) which significantly attenuates postprandial glucose response; (2) high fat content (11.2 g/100g) slowing gastric emptying; (3) dietary fibre from spinach (2.5 g/100g); (4) low net carbohydrate (4.2 g/100g). Analogous reference: mixed meat-and-vegetable curry dishes with similar macronutrient profiles reported GI 20–30 in peer-reviewed literature. GL per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (25 × 10.5) / 100 = 2.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (310 mg/100g) and saturated fat from cream, butter, and ghee
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g, request reduced salt and ghee preparation, and avoid pairing with high-sodium breads; the potassium content (350 mg/100g) from spinach provides a partial counterbalancing benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~25) driven by high protein, fat, and dietary fibre content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Saag Mutton is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low GI and high protein-to-carbohydrate ratio; a standard 250 g serving delivers only ~10.5 g of carbohydrate and a glycemic load of approximately 2.6, making it unlikely to cause significant postprandial glucose excursions when consumed without high-GI accompaniments.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from mutton, cream, and ghee; moderate sodium; but beneficial iron, potassium, and spinach-derived antioxidants
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate consumption to 1–2 servings per week, request preparation with minimal ghee or cream, choose lean mutton cuts, and benefit from the dish's high iron (3.8 mg/100g), potassium (350 mg/100g), and vitamin K (250 mcg/100g) content from spinach.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat as a potential pro-inflammatory trigger; spinach oxalate and purine content
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should monitor personal tolerance; the ginger and turmeric in the dish provide anti-inflammatory benefits, but high spinach intake may be problematic for those sensitive to oxalates or purines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Saag Mutton, also known as Mutton Saagwala or Palak Mutton, is a classic North Indian curry originating from the Punjab region. The dish combines slow-cooked, tender pieces of mutton — typically bone-in for depth of flavour — with a silky, vibrantly green gravy made from pureed or finely chopped spinach (saag), often enriched with mustard greens or fenugreek leaves. The mutton is first browned and braised with onions, ginger, garlic, tomatoes, and a blend of aromatic spices including cumin, coriander, turmeric, garam masala, and chili. The spinach base is separately blanched and pureed, then combined with the cooked mutton and simmered until the flavours meld. A finishing touch of cream, butter, or ghee rounds out the earthiness of the greens and the richness of the meat. The result is a dish with a deeply savoury, mildly spiced, and slightly earthy flavour profile, with a creamy, thick gravy that clings to the meat. Nutritionally, Saag Mutton is notable for its high protein content (~12.5 g/100g), significant iron (3.8 mg/100g), high vitamin A (450 mcg RAE/100g) and vitamin K (250 mcg/100g) from spinach, and vitamin B12 (1.5 mcg/100g) from mutton. Its very low glycemic index (~25) makes it suitable for blood sugar management. It is traditionally served with makki di roti (cornmeal flatbread), naan, or paratha, and is a cold-weather staple across North India and the Indian diaspora globally.
common_user_queries: Is saag mutton good for diabetics?
Is saag mutton high in iron?
meal_context: served with naan, paratha, or makki di roti

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN component-level data for cooked mutton and spinach-based Indian preparations; no single approved-source entry exists for Saag Mutton as a composite dish. GI is an analogous estimate. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for cooked mutton. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact composite dish entry was found in any single approved source.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation (monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat), cholesterol, and moisture data for Saag Mutton as a composite dish are not separately reported in ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for this specific preparation as of 2024; these fields are therefore marked as data not available. 2. saturated_fat_g (4.2 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked mutton (NDB 17174) and adjusted for cream/ghee contribution. 3. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Saag Mutton exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. Vitamin A reported as mcg RAE (450 mcg/100g) consistent with spinach-rich preparation; original input reported 450 mcg which is retained. 5. Portions in the input contained three identical duplicate entries (250g); deduplicated to a single default serving of 250g. 6. Halal flag set to true as mutton is permissible under Islamic dietary law when sourced from a halal-certified supplier; individual sourcing should be verified. 7. Punjabi alias ਸਾਗ ਮਟਨ is included in the flat aliases array to satisfy q8 consistency.
